Moses assures Israel of God’s constant presence with them. The apostle applies this to all of God's spiritual Israel, to encourage their faith and hope; the gospel has been preached to us as well as to them; he will never fail you, nor forsake you. Moses commends Joshua to them as a leader; someone whose wisdom, courage, and affection they had long known; someone whom God had appointed to be their leader; and therefore would acknowledge and bless. Joshua is pleased to be encouraged by Moses to be strong and of good courage. Those who have God with them will succeed well; therefore they ought to be of good courage. Through God let us do valiantly, for through him we will achieve victory; if we resist the devil, he will flee from us.

Though we read the word privately, we must not think it needless to hear it read publicly. This solemn reading of the law must be done in the year of release. The year of release was typical of gospel grace, which is called the acceptable year of the Lord; for our pardon and liberty by Christ, obligates us to keep his commandments. It must be read to all Israel, men, women, children, and strangers. It is God’s will that all people should become acquainted with his word. It is a rule for everyone, therefore it should be read to everyone. Whoever has read of the efforts many persons took to obtain scraps of Scripture when a whole copy could not be obtained or safely possessed, will see how thankful we should be for the thousands of copies among us. They will also understand the very different situation in which the Israelites were placed for many ages. But people’s hearts are so careless that all will be found too little to maintain knowledge of the truths, precepts, and worship of God.

Moses and Joshua attended the Divine Majesty at the door of the tabernacle. Moses is told again that he must soon die; even those who are most ready and willing to die need to be often reminded of its coming. The Lord tells Moses that after his death, the covenant he had taken so much pains to make between Israel and their God would certainly be broken. Israel would forsake him; then God would forsake Israel. Justly does he cast off those who so unjustly cast him off. Moses is directed to deliver them a song, which should remain a standing testimony for God, as faithful in giving them warning, and against them, as people false to themselves in not taking the warning. The word of God discerns the thoughts and intents of men's hearts, and meets them with reproofs and correction. Ministers who preach the word do not know the imaginations of men; but God, whose word it is, knows perfectly.

The solemn delivery of the book of the law to the Levites, to be deposited in or rather by the side of the ark, is related again. The song which follows in the next chapter is delivered to Moses, and by him to the people. He wrote it first, as the Holy Spirit taught him; and then spoke it in the hearing of all the people. Moses tells them plainly, I know that after my death you will utterly corrupt yourselves. Many a sad thought no doubt it occasioned to this good man; but his comfort was that he had done his duty, and that God would be glorified in their dispersion, if not in their settlement, for the foundation of God stands sure.
